title: ‘The Holdovers’ and ‘The Hunger Games.’ Plus: Eli Roth!
format: Two H movies and a slasher director. Amanda has a crisis of confidence over the new Hunger Games prequel, Sean falls hard but strangely for Alexander Payne's The Holdovers, and Eli Roth finally gets to talk about the Thanksgiving movie he's wanted to make since he was twelve.

segments.heading: The Hunger Games: The Ballad of Songbirds & Snakes
segments.note: Amanda's crisis, Sean's more measured take

films.title: The Hunger Games: The Ballad of Songbirds & Snakes
films.note: A prequel to the Jennifer Lawrence trilogy, following a young Coriolanus Snow (Tom Blythe) as he mentors Lucy Gray Baird (Rachel Zegler) in the 10th Hunger Games. Amanda thought it fucking blew: bloated world-building, a Lumineers-style singing subplot she loathed, and a Zegler-Blythe romance with no chemistry. Sean thought the first two-thirds worked as a straight Hunger Games movie but the final third, once Snow gets banished, goes bland and confusing. Both agree Viola Davis and Jason Schwartzman are having a great time chewing scenery.

films.title: Constantine
films.note: Cited to make the point that director Francis Lawrence has been making slick studio genre movies for 20 years, so incompetence isn't what's driving the movie's problems.

films.title: Slumberland
films.note: Lawrence's Netflix film that "not a single person saw," part of why he needed this one to hit.

films.title: West Side Story
films.note: Sean's reminder that Zegler was genuinely great here; the film itself just went unseen.

films.title: Shazam! Fury of the Gods
films.note: Filed under one of the worst movies of the year, part of the rough stretch Zegler's been navigating.

segments.heading: The Holdovers
segments.note: Alexander Payne's first film in six years

films.title: The Holdovers
films.note: Paul Giamatti plays a prep-school classics teacher stuck minding the students who can't go home for Christmas break, alongside Da'Vine Joy Randolph as the school's grieving cook and newcomer Dominic Sessa as the one kid left behind. Sean loved it: he laughed, cried, calls Sessa a real discovery and Giamatti one of the best actors alive. Amanda admired it without connecting to it, and can't quite pin down why. To her it's a well-made, well-acted Christmas dramedy that plays like a deliberate throwback to 1970s character pieces, without the bigger idea she thinks Payne is usually chasing.

films.title: Harold and Maude
films.note: The film Payne himself is most associated with championing, and the clearest tonal ancestor: bone-dry, sad, funny.

films.title: The Last Detail
films.note: Another 70s touchstone Sean names for the film's mismatched-trio, bittersweet register.

films.title: Dead Poets Society
films.note: The prep-school comparison that keeps coming up ("O Captain, My Captain"); Amanda has real affection for it.

films.title: With Honors
films.note: One of three 90s prep-school movies (outsider enters a high-society institution) that Amanda says hit her at exactly the right age.

films.title: School Ties
films.note: Grouped with With Honors and Outside Providence as Amanda's formative 90s prep-school run.

films.title: Outside Providence
films.note: The third in that same run of 90s prep-school movies.

films.title: Make Way for Tomorrow
films.note: Leo McCarey's film about an elderly couple losing their home and being split up by their grown children, which Payne recommended as a companion piece. Sean watched it for the first time this week and calls it one of the saddest movies he's ever seen. He says it retroactively deepened his read on The Holdovers as a movie about parents and the people who stand in for them.

segments.heading: Where this sits in the Payne filmography
segments.note: and whether it needed a bigger idea

films.title: Election
films.note: Sean's favorite Payne film made in his lifetime, anchored by Tracy Flick, a character he sees a version of in himself.

films.title: Sideways
films.note: Payne and Giamatti's first collaboration; The Holdovers is billed as their reunion.

films.title: Nebraska
films.note: Sean's other favorite: a small story about overlooked, aging people in the middle of the country that carries a bigger idea about who America casts aside.

films.title: Downsizing
films.note: Payne's prior film and a commercial bomb; Sean rewatched it for this episode and still finds it a bold, ambitious sci-fi farce whose relationships never quite gel, even though its environmental themes do.

films.title: The Descendants
films.note: The Payne film Sean likes least, though he still likes it fine, and the one the Academy loved most before The Holdovers.

films.title: Citizen Ruth
films.note: Invoked as a reminder of how much bitterer and stranger Payne's early work was compared to this one.

segments.heading: Sincerity, awards chances, and the rest of the field
segments.note: Belfast, Licorice Pizza, and what makes a movie feel sincere

films.title: Belfast
films.note: Sean's awards-season comparison point: a lovely, sentimental favorite he liked but didn't think should win Best Picture, similar to how he expects The Holdovers to be received.

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: Named as the kind of more audacious filmmaking Sean is hungry for after a movie as reserved as The Holdovers.

films.title: The Color Purple
films.note: Unseen at recording time, but flagged as another likely Best Picture contender that, unlike The Holdovers, probably won't make audiences feel good.

films.title: Frances Ha
films.note: One of Amanda's favorite films and her go-to example of a sincere movie made in the last 30 years: limited scope, young woman at the center, an obvious point of comparison for The Holdovers.

films.title: Arrival
films.note: Sean's pick for a sincere modern film; Amanda loves it too.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: Paul Thomas Anderson's deliberate step back in scale and ambition, which Sean thinks lands emotionally in a way that hits him harder than The Holdovers does.

segments.heading: Eli Roth on Thanksgiving
segments.note: the slasher he's wanted to make since he was twelve

films.title: Thanksgiving
films.note: Roth's holiday slasher, built out from the fake trailer he made for Grindhouse. He and co-writer Jeff Rendell grew up in Massachusetts obsessed with the genre and treated the movie's inciting Black Friday stampede and mystery-killer structure like a real whodunit, workshopping every kill and every Boston accent for years before finally shooting it.

films.title: Grindhouse
films.note: The Tarantino/Rodriguez double feature that housed Roth's original Thanksgiving fake trailer. It was a bomb at the time, but the trailer became the thing fans kept asking him to actually make.

films.title: The Green Inferno
films.note: Roth's cannibal movie, delayed a full year by its distributor before release, part of why he stepped back from pure horror for a decade.

films.title: Knock Knock
films.note: A movie Roth felt was mishandled on release but that quietly became a Netflix hit afterward.

films.title: Death Wish
films.note: His Bruce Willis remake, taken almost on impulse after another project fell through, the start of him deliberately branching out of horror.

films.title: The House with a Clock in Its Walls
films.note: His PG family horror movie with Cate Blanchett and Jack Black, made to prove he could control tone and production design on a bigger budget.

films.title: Inglourious Basterds
films.note: Roth's acting turn for Tarantino, cited as the experience that helped prepare him to work with major stars.

films.title: Borderlands
films.note: His next film, mentioned twice, shot during COVID with Cate Blanchett, Jack Black, and Kevin Hart, more logistically challenging than he expected.

films.title: Arthur the King
films.note: The Mark Wahlberg adventure-race drama Roth says he produced, which Sean had texted him about without realizing Roth was involved.

films.title: Imaginary
films.note: The upcoming Blumhouse horror-comedy about a girl's creepy imaginary friend, whose trailer Sean found genuinely funny.

films.title: M3GAN
films.note: Cited twice as recent horror Roth actually enjoyed, distinct from viral-gimmick horror he's less interested in.

films.title: Scream
films.note: Roth's standard for a slasher with no logical cheats: a movie that earns its reputation by following its own rules to the end.

films.title: The Ring
films.note: His other example of a horror movie that holds together because it's well written all the way through.

films.title: Sleepaway Camp
films.note: The movie whose ending made him scream and jump out of his chair; he calls the finale legendary.

films.title: Pieces
films.note: His favorite slasher film, over-the-top and ridiculous but, to him, exactly what Texas Chain Saw Massacre's kills promised and didn't fully deliver.

films.title: The Texas Chain Saw Massacre
films.note: The film Pieces' chainsaw scenes were, for young Roth, a more satisfying answer to.

films.title: My Bloody Valentine
films.note: Named as one of the slashers that actually plays fair as a whodunit.

films.title: Happy Birthday to Me
films.note: Grouped with the whodunit-slasher tradition Roth says is underrated and hard to pull off.

films.title: The Prowler
films.note: Another entry in Roth's rundown of guessing-game slashers.

films.title: The Mutilator
films.note: Roth's example of a slasher that doesn't even try to be clever (a father just methodically kills everyone), and he says he appreciates that honesty too.

films.title: Mute Witness
films.note: A largely forgotten British thriller Roth calls one of the best cat-and-mouse slashers ever made; he recently caught a rare 35mm screening.

films.title: April Fool's Day
films.note: One of Roth's favorite holiday slashers, part of the lineage he's tapping with Thanksgiving.

films.title: Black Christmas
films.note: Named as the film that effectively started the holiday-slasher subgenre.

films.title: Halloween
films.note: The genre's biggest touchstone in that same holiday-slasher lineage.

films.title: New Year's Evil
films.note: Another holiday slasher Roth cites in tracing the subgenre before Thanksgiving.

films.title: Silent Night, Deadly Night
films.note: A formative theatrical experience for Roth as a kid. He calls it extremely fucked up and rapturous, and recently rewatched it at a New Beverly screening.

films.title: Torso
films.note: One of his favorite giallo films, praised for how it plays with red herrings alongside the killer's own point of view.

films.title: Planet Terror
films.note: Rodriguez's half of Grindhouse, part of the double feature Roth's original Thanksgiving trailer ran inside.

films.title: Death Proof
films.note: Tarantino's half of that same Grindhouse bill.

films.title: Investigation of a Citizen Above Suspicion
films.note: Roth's answer for the last great thing he's seen: a serious, dry Elio Petri film shot by Luigi Kuveiller, whom Roth admires for his early giallo work before moving to prestige films.

films.title: The 10th Victim
films.note: His other recent watch, a stylish 60s sci-fi black comedy about a televised kill-or-be-killed game. Roth calls the production design and Ursula Andress-vs-Marcello Mastroianni premise a total pleasure.

films.title: Deep Red
films.note: Cited as one of the films DP Luigi Kuveiller shot before moving into prestige work.

films.title: The New York Ripper
films.note: Another Kuveiller-shot giallo Roth calls one of his favorites.

films.title: A Lizard in a Woman's Skin
films.note: The Fulci film Roth cites for Florinda Bolkan's performance, made right before her turn in Investigation of a Citizen Above Suspicion.

films.title: Toby Dammit
films.note: Fellini's segment Roth name-checks while tracing the same DP's early filmography.
